items.prompt: How does LLM-based synthesis typically differ from GAN-based synthesis?
choices: LLMs always require paired image domains
LLMs condition a pretrained model on prompts or schema instructions to emit new text or records
LLMs eliminate the need for any validation
items.explain: Section 10.5 contrasts adversarial GAN training with prompt-conditioned LLM emission of text, labels, or rows.

items.prompt: LLM-generated synthetic text can hallucinate facts and still require screening before training use.
items.explain: Section 10.5 lists hallucination as a key risk requiring validation in regulated domains.

items.prompt: Which workflow appears in both LLM synthesis and weak supervision?
choices: Model-proposed labels later audited by humans or heuristics
Mandatory deletion of all real data
items.explain: Section 10.5 notes LLMs proposing candidate labels that humans or heuristics later audit.

items.prompt: Name two risks of LLM synthetic data besides hallucination.
items.rubric: Mention memorization/leakage of PII and/or social bias from pretraining corpora.
items.sample_answer: Memorization can leak training snippets or PII, and pretraining bias can skew generated text or labels.

items.prompt: Synthetic LLM outputs should pass the same validation and ethics gates as GAN or statistical synthesis.
items.explain: Section 10.5 states LLM generation must pass the same validation and ethics gates as other synthesis methods.
